content.summary: Static analysis means examining a file's structure and content without running it — keeping you safe while uncovering what the software can do.
content.explanation: Static analysis is the practice of reading a program's bytes, structure, and metadata without ever executing the code. Unlike dynamic analysis (running the file in a sandbox), static analysis carries essentially zero risk of infection. Anya reads the file's headers, imports, strings, and entropy — none of which requires execution. The trade-off: static analysis cannot observe behaviour that only manifests at runtime, such as decryption of an encrypted payload or network communication. Think of it as reading a recipe rather than tasting the dish.
content.what_to_look_for: Start with the Overview tab: look at the risk score, the file format, and whether security mitigations (ASLR, DEP) are enabled. Then move to Imports — the import table tells you what the file *can* do without running it.
content.real_world_example: When WannaCry was discovered in 2017, static analysis immediately revealed its import of the EternalBlue exploit DLL and its ransomware-typical file enumeration and encryption APIs — all without running it on a single machine.
content.next_action: Drop a file onto Anya and look at the Overview tab. Note the risk score and the file format before exploring the other tabs.

glossary.definition: Examining a program without executing it.
Running a program in a controlled environment to observe its behaviour.
A list of external functions a program calls from other DLLs or shared libraries.

content.summary: PE (Portable Executable) is the file format used for .exe, .dll, and .sys files on Windows. It organises code, data, and metadata into sections with a structured header.
content.explanation: Every Windows executable follows the PE format: a DOS header (legacy), a PE signature, a COFF header describing the machine type and section count, an optional header with the entry point and security flags, a section table describing each chunk of the file, and then the section data itself. The most important sections are .text (code), .data (initialised globals), .rdata (read-only data, including the import/export tables), and .rsrc (resources). Malware often uses unusual section names, and packers frequently replace all sections with a stub loader and an encrypted payload.
content.what_to_look_for: Check the Sections tab. Look at section names, sizes, and entropy. Normal .text sections have moderate entropy (4–6). Entropy above 7.5 in an executable section usually means encrypted or packed code.
content.real_world_example: UPX-packed malware typically has two sections named 'UPX0' and 'UPX1' — instantly recognisable even before any dynamic analysis.
content.next_action: Open the Sections tab and look at the section names and entropy values. Does anything stand out as unusual?

glossary.definition: Portable Executable — the binary format for Windows programs.
The code section of a PE file.
A named region of a PE file with specific permissions (readable, writable, executable).
The address where execution begins when the program loads.

content.summary: ELF (Executable and Linkable Format) is the standard binary format for Linux, Android, and many Unix-like systems.
content.explanation: ELF files consist of an ELF header (magic bytes, architecture, entry point), program headers (segments loaded by the OS), and section headers (logical divisions like .text, .data, .bss). Unlike PE files, ELF uses a different security model: PIE (Position Independent Executable) is the ELF equivalent of ASLR, RELRO protects the GOT from overwrites, and the NX bit on the stack prevents shellcode execution. ELF binaries typically link against libc and other shared libraries via the GOT/PLT mechanism.
content.what_to_look_for: Check whether PIE, NX stack, and RELRO are enabled. Look at the interpreter path — unusual paths are a red flag. Check for stripped symbols, which hinder reverse engineering.
content.real_world_example: Mirai's ELF binaries for ARM and MIPS had all symbols stripped, imported mprotect and execve (for shellcode execution), and connected to a hardcoded C2 IP — all visible through static analysis.
content.next_action: Look at the Security tab for PIE/NX/RELRO status. Then check the Imports tab for suspicious libc functions.

glossary.definition: Executable and Linkable Format — the binary format for Linux and Unix-like systems.
Position Independent Executable — allows ASLR to randomise the base address.
Global Offset Table / Procedure Linkage Table — the mechanism ELF uses for calling shared library functions.
RELocation Read-Only — makes the GOT non-writable after linking, preventing GOT overwrites.

content.summary: Anya's risk score (0–100) is an additive indicator, not a verdict. A high score means many suspicious indicators were found; a low score means few indicators — not that the file is safe.
content.explanation: The risk score is calculated by summing weighted contributions: Critical findings add 25 points, High findings add 15, Medium add 8, and Low add 3. Structural findings like packer detection (+20), TLS callbacks (+10), and high-entropy overlay (+15) add additional weight. The score is capped at 100. Scores 0–29 are low risk, 30–59 medium, 60–79 high, and 80–100 critical. Important caveat: a score of 0 does not mean the file is safe — it means Anya found no indicators it was looking for. Novel malware, well-obfuscated files, and files that trigger nothing in the static signature set will score 0.
content.what_to_look_for: Look beyond the score at the individual findings. Two files with a score of 60 might be entirely different threats.
content.real_world_example: Stuxnet scored very high because it imported dozens of suspicious APIs, had a packed section, used TLS callbacks, and contained an encrypted payload overlay — every category contributed.
content.next_action: Read the detection tags below the score on the Overview tab. Each tag corresponds to a specific indicator found in this file.

glossary.definition: A 0–100 summary of how many and how severe the suspicious indicators found in a file are.
A legitimate file flagged as suspicious because it uses APIs or techniques also used by malware.
A malicious file that scores low because it uses techniques not in the signature set.

content.summary: Shannon entropy measures the randomness of data on a 0–8 scale. Packed or encrypted content has high entropy; normal code has moderate entropy.
content.explanation: Shannon entropy, developed by Claude Shannon in 1948, measures the average information content per byte in a sequence. For binary analysis, we measure it on a 0–8 scale (0 = completely uniform, 8 = perfectly random). Think of it like this: a dictionary page has low entropy because the same letters and words repeat constantly. White noise from a radio has high entropy because every sample is unpredictable. Compiled code has moderate entropy (4–6) because there are patterns in instruction sequences. Packed or encrypted data looks like random noise and measures 7.5–8.0. A section with entropy above 7.5 almost certainly contains encrypted or compressed data.
content.what_to_look_for: Open the Entropy tab. Look for sections or file-level entropy above 7.5. If the overall file entropy is high but there are few imports, the file is probably packed.
content.real_world_example: Every time Emotet updated its packing layer, the packed section entropy would be 7.9–8.0 — a consistent forensic indicator even when the hash changed daily.
content.next_action: Open the Entropy tab and note the file-level entropy value. Is it above 6.5? Above 7.5?

glossary.definition: A measure of the randomness or unpredictability of data, expressed on a 0–8 scale for binary files.
Compressing or encrypting a binary so its true content is only visible after the packer stub decrypts it in memory at runtime.

title: High Entropy Sections — What They Mean

content.summary: One or more sections in this file have unusually high entropy, suggesting encrypted or compressed content that hides the real code from static analysis.
content.explanation: High entropy in a PE section (above 7.0) almost always indicates one of three things: the section contains a compressed resource (like a PNG image), the section contains an encrypted payload that will be decrypted at runtime, or the file is packed and the section is the compressed original binary. The most suspicious scenario is a high-entropy section that is also marked executable — this is the classic packed malware pattern. A packer stub in a normal-entropy .text section decrypts the real code into a high-entropy section, then jumps to it.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at which specific section has high entropy. Is it executable? Does the entry point fall in that section? Are the imports suspiciously few (< 5 functions from 1–2 DLLs)?
content.real_world_example: IcedID (BokBot) consistently uses a high-entropy packed section with a small stub loader. The import table shows only 3–4 functions — far fewer than the actual capabilities the unpacked code would reveal.
content.next_action: Check the Sections tab. Look for sections marked with a high entropy warning. Note whether the entry point is in the first or a later section.

glossary.definition: A PE section with the IMAGE_SCN_MEM_EXECUTE flag, meaning the CPU can run code from it.
A binary where the original code is compressed or encrypted and a stub loader unpacks it at runtime.
Anya flags sections as suspicious when Shannon entropy exceeds 7.5.

content.summary: This file appears to be packed. Static analysis of packed files is limited — the real import table and code are only visible after unpacking.
content.explanation: A packed binary has been compressed or encrypted so that the original code is hidden. What you see in Anya's import table is just the packer stub's imports — a tiny fraction of what the unpacked binary would reveal. The typical static analysis limitation: you can confirm packing, identify the packer (if it's a known one like UPX), and note the entropy of sections, but you cannot see the real API calls until the binary is unpacked. For UPX-packed files, unpacking is trivial: use 'upx -d filename.exe'. For custom packers, you'll need dynamic analysis (run in a sandbox or unpack manually in a debugger).
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the packer detection result in the Overview tab. Check section names for packer signatures (UPX0/UPX1, .vmp0, .themida). Note the import count — packed files usually import fewer than 5 functions.
content.real_world_example: The Mirai botnet spread as UPX-packed ELF binaries. Unpacking with 'upx -d' immediately revealed the full import table including connect, socket, and send — the C2 communication functions.
content.next_action: If this is a UPX-packed file, try: upx -d suspicious.exe (Linux/macOS) or use a tool like CFF Explorer on Windows. Then re-analyse the unpacked file in Anya.

glossary.definition: Ultimate Packer for eXecutables — a free, open-source executable packer widely used by both legitimate software and malware.
The unpacking code that runs first in a packed binary, decrypts the real payload, and jumps to it.
After unpacking, many more imports become visible that were hidden by the packer.

content.summary: The import table lists every external function a program calls. It's the single most revealing piece of information in static analysis.
content.explanation: Windows programs don't contain all the code they use. Instead, they import functions from Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs) — shared libraries that Windows provides. The import table lists every DLL and every function the program calls from that DLL. This directly reveals capabilities: if a program imports CreateRemoteThread, it can inject code into other processes. If it imports InternetOpen, it can make HTTP requests. If it imports SetWindowsHookEx, it can intercept all keystrokes. The import table is the single most revealing artefact in static analysis — more so than the code itself for quick triage.
content.what_to_look_for: Open the Imports tab. Look for functions grouped under kernel32.dll (low-level process/memory operations), ntdll.dll (direct NT API — more suspicious), wininet.dll or winhttp.dll (networking), and advapi32.dll (registry/services/tokens).
content.real_world_example: When analysts saw that a suspicious file imported OpenProcess + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread + VirtualAllocEx together, they immediately classified it as a process injector before reading a single instruction of assembly.
content.next_action: Open the Imports tab. Are there any red-highlighted suspicious APIs? Click on a suspicious API badge to see which MITRE ATT&CK technique it maps to.

glossary.definition: Dynamic Link Library — a shared library on Windows that multiple programs can use simultaneously.
Import Address Table — the in-memory version of the import table, filled in by the Windows loader.
An API reference by number instead of name, used to hide the function being called.

title: Process Injection APIs — Step by Step

content.summary: This file contains the three-API process injection combo: VirtualAllocEx + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread.
content.explanation: Classic process injection works in three steps: (1) VirtualAllocEx — allocate a region of memory inside a *different* running process, specifying PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE to make it both writable and executable. (2) WriteProcessMemory — copy shellcode or a DLL path into that allocated region. (3) CreateRemoteThread — create a thread inside the target process, with the start address pointing to the code just written. The result: your code runs inside a legitimate process like explorer.exe or svchost.exe, inheriting its network permissions and evading process-based detection. No file is written to disk for the payload itself.
content.what_to_look_for: Look for a target process hint in the Strings tab — malware often hardcodes 'explorer.exe', 'svchost.exe', or 'lsass.exe' as injection targets. Also check for OpenProcess (needed before VirtualAllocEx) in the import table.
content.real_world_example: Cobalt Strike's default process injection technique uses exactly this combo, often targeting notepad.exe or svchost.exe. The process can be identified by looking for CreateToolhelp32Snapshot (process enumeration to find the target) alongside the injection trio.
content.next_action: Check the Strings tab for hardcoded process names. Look for CreateToolhelp32Snapshot — used to enumerate running processes to find the injection target.

glossary.definition: Executing code inside another process's address space to evade detection and inherit permissions.
A memory protection flag making a region both writable and executable — very unusual in legitimate code.
A thread created inside a different process, as opposed to within the same process.

content.summary: This file imports some functions by number (ordinal) rather than by name, hiding which APIs it calls from basic static analysis tools.
content.explanation: Normally, the import table lists function names like 'CreateRemoteThread'. With ordinal imports, instead of a name, you see only a number — e.g., ntdll.dll ordinal #12. To know which function ordinal #12 maps to, you need to look up the export table of ntdll.dll from the exact Windows version the malware targets. This is a deliberate evasion technique: signature-based tools that scan for API names will miss ordinal-imported functions entirely.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at which DLL has ordinal imports. Ordinals from ntdll.dll or kernel32.dll are more concerning than from user32.dll or shell32.dll.
content.real_world_example: Some variants of PlugX and Turla used ntdll ordinal imports for their process injection routines specifically to bypass string-based AV signatures that looked for 'NtCreateThreadEx' in the import table.
content.next_action: Note the DLL name and ordinal number. To resolve it, download the matching DLL from a trusted Windows installation and use a tool like Ghidra, PE-bear, or CFF Explorer to view its exports.

glossary.definition: An import referenced by number rather than by name.
The numeric identifier for a function in a DLL's export table.

content.summary: Different DLLs reveal different capabilities. Knowing what each DLL is used for lets you quickly assess what a binary can do.
content.explanation: Key DLLs and what they reveal: kernel32.dll — core process, memory, file, and thread operations. ntdll.dll — direct NT kernel API (more powerful and lower-level than kernel32; ntdll imports are more suspicious because they can bypass user-mode hooks). wininet.dll / winhttp.dll — HTTP/HTTPS networking. ws2_32.dll — raw TCP/UDP sockets. advapi32.dll — registry, services, cryptography, security tokens. shell32.dll — shell operations, file associations. psapi.dll / toolhelp32 — process enumeration (reconnaissance). A file importing only kernel32.dll + ntdll.dll with no other DLLs may be trying to minimise its detectable surface.
content.what_to_look_for: Count the DLLs. Legitimate applications typically import from 5–15 DLLs. Fewer than 3 (especially in an apparently complex program) suggests packing or ordinal-only imports.
content.real_world_example: Early Remcos RAT samples imported only from kernel32.dll, ntdll.dll, and ws2_32.dll — three DLLs giving it full process control plus raw socket networking, with a minimal import footprint.
content.next_action: Look at the DLL list in the Imports tab. Are there any unexpected DLLs? Is the DLL count unusually low?

glossary.definition: The NT layer DLL — provides direct access to Windows NT system calls, bypassing higher-level user-mode hooks.
The Windows Sockets 2 DLL — used for TCP/UDP socket networking.

content.summary: This file calls APIs to check whether it is being run inside a debugger, a common anti-analysis technique.
content.explanation: The most common approach is IsDebuggerPresent — a single API that returns TRUE if the calling process is being debugged. Malware typically calls this early and exits cleanly (or loads a decoy) if it returns TRUE. CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent can detect debuggers attached from another process. NtQueryInformationProcess with ProcessDebugPort is a lower-level version that bypasses some patching. More sophisticated techniques check the BeingDebugged flag in the PEB directly (without an API call), use timing checks (execution under a debugger is slower), and check for hardware breakpoints via debug registers.
content.what_to_look_for: Look for IsDebuggerPresent AND a conditional early-exit pattern (usually followed by ExitProcess or a clean-looking return). Also check if there are timing APIs (GetTickCount, QueryPerformanceCounter) nearby — timing anti-debug is often combined with API-based checks.
content.real_world_example: Banking trojan Dridex patched its own IsDebuggerPresent to always return FALSE — it detected and patched the analysis tool check before the analyst could patch it back. Only hardware debugger detection (checking debug registers) could bypass this.
content.next_action: Note the full set of anti-analysis indicators in the Overview tab. Is this the only check, or are there VM checks and timing checks as well? Multiple methods suggest a more sophisticated evasion strategy.

glossary.definition: Windows API that returns TRUE if the current process has a debugger attached.
Process Environment Block — an in-memory structure containing process state, including a BeingDebugged flag.
A popular plugin for x64dbg that patches out common debugger detection techniques.

content.summary: This file may attempt to detect whether it is running inside a virtual machine or analysis sandbox, a technique used to evade automated analysis.
content.explanation: VMs and sandboxes leave many artefacts: registry keys (HKLM\SOFTWARE\VMware, HKLM\SOFTWARE\VBOX), process names (vmwaretray.exe, vboxservice.exe), MAC address prefixes (00:0C:29 for VMware, 08:00:27 for VirtualBox), CPUID leaf 0x40000000 (hypervisor vendor string), unusual disk names, and low uptime. The GetSystemFirmwareTable API retrieves ACPI/SMBIOS data — VM-hosted firmware has distinctive strings (VMware, VBOX, QEMU). Malware that detects it's in a sandbox typically terminates, sleeps for a very long time, or executes a benign code path.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the Strings tab for VBOX, VMWARE, QEMU, or SANDBOX strings. Also check the anti-analysis indicators section in the Overview tab.
content.real_world_example: Emotet stopped executing in environments where the computer name was 'DESKTOP-' followed by a standard pattern, where the username was 'admin' or 'analyst', or where there were fewer than 100 files in common directories — all sandbox indicators.
content.next_action: Run this file in a heavily customised sandbox (non-standard username, realistic file count, physical hardware MAC addresses) or on bare metal in an isolated environment.

glossary.definition: A CPU instruction that returns processor and hypervisor identification — detectable from software.
System Management BIOS — firmware tables that contain hardware identifiers, often with VM-specific values.
A real physical machine as opposed to a virtual machine — harder for malware to detect.

content.summary: This file has TLS (Thread Local Storage) callbacks — functions that execute before the program's entry point, before most debuggers can intercept them.
content.explanation: Thread Local Storage callbacks are executed by the Windows loader before calling the program's entry point (main/WinMain). This means: before most debuggers attach at 'entry point', before many sandbox hooks are in place, and before the analyst's first breakpoint fires. Malware uses TLS callbacks for: checking for debuggers and exiting before analysis begins, decrypting the real payload that is then executed at the normal entry point, unpacking the import table, and running anti-analysis code that modifies the binary in memory before it appears to start.
content.what_to_look_for: Note the callback addresses shown in Anya's JSON output. In x64dbg, set a breakpoint on every TLS callback VirtualAddress before running the file. The entry point you see in the Overview tab is where *main* starts, not where TLS callbacks start.
content.real_world_example: Some Delphi-compiled malware families use TLS callbacks as their primary anti-debug mechanism — the real IsDebuggerPresent check happens in TLS, long before the debugger-visible entry point is reached.
content.next_action: In x64dbg: Options → Preferences → Events → check 'TLS Callbacks'. This ensures x64dbg breaks at every TLS callback before entry.

glossary.definition: Thread Local Storage — a mechanism for per-thread data, whose callbacks are called before program entry.
A function registered in the TLS directory that Windows calls before the program's main entry point.
A popular open-source debugger for 64-bit Windows binaries.

title: Data After the Last Section — Overlay

content.summary: This file has data appended after its last PE section — a pattern used by droppers to hide encrypted payloads from section-based analysis.
content.explanation: The PE format only defines sections up to a certain file offset. Any bytes after the last section's raw data end are called an 'overlay' and are ignored by the Windows loader. Legitimate software does use overlays: self-extracting archives (WinRAR/7-Zip), digitally signed PE files (the signature sits in an overlay), and some installers. For malware, overlays are a convenient place to store an encrypted second-stage payload, configuration data (C2 addresses, encryption keys), or an embedded file to drop to disk. The overlay's entropy indicates whether the data is encrypted (high entropy) or plaintext (low entropy).
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the overlay entropy in Anya's JSON output. High entropy (> 6.5) suggests encrypted content. Note the offset and size — you can extract it with: dd if=suspicious.exe bs=1 skip=OFFSET count=SIZE of=overlay.bin
content.real_world_example: Many Dridex samples carried their encrypted configuration blob as a 4–8 KB overlay at the end of an otherwise legitimate-looking PE. The overlay entropy was consistently 7.9–8.0.
content.next_action: If overlay entropy is high: extract the overlay bytes using the offset/size from Anya's JSON output. Try to determine the file type (check for magic bytes), look for repeated patterns suggesting an XOR key, and submit to a sandbox.

glossary.definition: Data appended after the last PE section — ignored by the Windows loader but accessible to the program via file I/O.
The first few bytes of a file that identify its format (e.g., 'MZ' for PE, 'PK' for ZIP).

title: ASLR and DEP — Why They Matter

content.summary: ASLR and DEP are security mitigations that make exploiting memory corruption bugs significantly harder. Their absence is a suspicious indicator.
content.explanation: ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomisation) randomises where code, stack, and heap are loaded in memory, making it harder for exploit code to jump to a known address. In PE files, ASLR is enabled by the IMAGE_DLLCHARACTERISTICS_DYNAMIC_BASE flag in the optional header. DEP (Data Execution Prevention) / NX marks data pages as non-executable, preventing shellcode from running in stack or heap memory. Enabled with IMAGE_DLLCHARACTERISTICS_NX_COMPAT. Legitimate software compiled with modern toolchains (MSVC, GCC, Clang) enables both by default. A PE without these flags was either compiled with a very old toolchain, deliberately stripped of them to improve exploit reliability, or is a handcrafted binary.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the Security tab. Note which flags are disabled. A PE missing both ASLR and DEP is an immediate red flag unless it's clearly a very old legacy binary.
content.real_world_example: Early Wannacry samples had DEP disabled — a relic of the exploit code's origin in the EternalBlue exploit that assumed an NX-disabled environment.
content.next_action: Check the Security tab. If both ASLR and DEP are disabled, combine this with the overall risk score for assessment.

glossary.definition: Address Space Layout Randomisation — randomises the load address of code and data to hinder exploitation.
Data Execution Prevention / No-eXecute — prevents code from running in data memory regions.
A field in the PE optional header that encodes security feature flags.

content.summary: MITRE ATT&CK is a globally-accessible knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) observed in real-world attacks.
content.explanation: MITRE ATT&CK organises adversary behaviour into 14 Tactics (high-level goals like Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Defense Evasion) and hundreds of Techniques and Sub-techniques (specific methods). Each technique has a unique ID (e.g., T1055.001 for Process Injection: Dynamic-link Library Injection) with documented procedure examples, mitigations, and detection guidance. When Anya identifies an API like CreateRemoteThread, it maps it to T1055.001 — immediately placing the finding in the context of real-world threat intelligence. SOC analysts use ATT&CK IDs in incident reports to communicate precisely what behaviour was observed.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the technique IDs in the Imports tab badges and the MITRE section of the JSON output. Click a badge to open the ATT&CK website page for that technique.
content.real_world_example: When APT29 (Cozy Bear) was tracked, security researchers used ATT&CK to map their observed TTPs: T1055 (Process Injection), T1547.001 (Registry Run Keys), T1003.001 (LSASS Dumping). The shared vocabulary allowed teams worldwide to compare findings.
content.next_action: Click on an ATT&CK badge in the Imports tab to open the ATT&CK website page for that technique. Look at the 'Procedure Examples' section — it lists real malware families that use that technique.

glossary.definition: A knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques used in real cyberattacks, maintained by MITRE Corporation.
Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures — the ways adversaries operate.
A specific implementation of a broader technique, identified by a decimal suffix (e.g., T1055.001).

content.summary: Each ATT&CK technique page is a wealth of threat intelligence — it lists real malware families, detection methods, and mitigations.
content.explanation: An ATT&CK technique page has several sections: Description (what the technique does and why), Procedure Examples (real malware families and how they used it — with citations to threat intelligence reports), Mitigations (how to prevent it), Detection (what to look for in logs and artefacts), and References (links to the research reports). The Procedure Examples section is especially valuable for matching what you found in the file to known threat actors.
content.what_to_look_for: Click a badge in Anya's Imports tab. On the ATT&CK page, go to 'Procedure Examples'. Do any named threat actors or malware families match what you know about the suspected source of this file?
content.real_world_example: T1003.001 (LSASS Memory) lists Mimikatz as a procedure example with a citation to the original research. If you find MinidumpWriteDump in a sample, you can immediately check which other groups have used the same technique.
content.next_action: Visit the ATT&CK technique page for the highest-confidence technique found in this file. Read the Procedure Examples and note if any described malware families match this sample's other indicators.

glossary.definition: A documented real-world use of an ATT&CK technique by a specific threat actor or malware family.
An individual, group, or nation-state conducting cyberattacks.

content.summary: A structured workflow for triaging a suspicious file from first look to escalation decision.
content.explanation: The standard triage sequence: (1) Hashes — check MD5/SHA256 on MalwareBazaar, VirusTotal, and your threat intelligence platform. Known-bad hash = instant escalation. (2) Entropy — is it packed? If entropy > 7.5, unpacking is needed before meaningful analysis. (3) Imports — what can it do? Look for injection, persistence, network, and anti-analysis APIs. (4) Strings — what did the developer leave behind? IP addresses, domains, registry paths, file paths, error messages, and PDB paths are all useful. (5) Security flags — does it have ASLR/DEP? (6) Risk score + MITRE techniques — what is the overall assessment? (7) Escalate if risk score > 60, or if any Critical-confidence finding is present.
content.what_to_look_for: Work through Anya's tabs in order: Overview (hashes, score) → Entropy → Imports → Strings → Security → Export the JSON for your ticket.
content.real_world_example: SOC analysts at a financial institution used exactly this workflow to triage 47 suspicious email attachments in 4 hours during a targeted phishing campaign, correctly identifying 3 as novel banking trojans and 44 as known commodity loaders.
content.next_action: Use Anya's JSON export to create a triage summary. Include: file hash, risk score, top 3 suspicious imports, MITRE technique IDs, and your escalation recommendation.

glossary.definition: The process of quickly assessing a suspicious file to determine its priority and next action.
A free public repository of malware samples and hashes maintained by abuse.ch.
A path to a debugging symbols file embedded in the PE — often reveals the attacker's development machine directory structure.

content.summary: Knowing when to escalate from triage to full incident response is a critical analyst skill. Here are the thresholds.
content.explanation: Escalate immediately (to IR team) if: any Critical-confidence finding is present (process injection combo, LSASS dump capability), risk score > 70, novel malware (no hash match on any threat intelligence platform), evidence of targeting (custom implant, PDB path with internal project names, strings in the target's language), or active infection indicators (the file was found running or established persistence). Continue static analysis if: medium-confidence findings only, known commodity malware (hash match found), no evidence of execution. Move to dynamic analysis (sandbox) if: file is packed (static analysis is limited), anti-analysis techniques detected, need to observe network communication or dropped files.
content.what_to_look_for: Review the MITRE techniques and confidence scores in the JSON output. Any Critical finding → escalate. Risk score > 70 without a known-good explanation → escalate.
content.real_world_example: A SOC analyst found a file with risk score 85, MinidumpWriteDump (Critical), and a PDB path containing 'lsass_dump_tool' — immediate IR escalation led to finding 12 compromised machines with credential theft activity.
content.next_action: Write a one-paragraph triage summary: 'This file is a [PE/ELF], risk score [N]. It imports [top 3 suspicious APIs] suggesting [capability]. MITRE techniques: [IDs]. Confidence: [level]. Recommendation: [action].'

glossary.definition: Incident Response — the structured process of detecting, containing, and recovering from a security incident.
A previously unseen malware sample with no hash match in any threat intelligence database.
Indicator of Compromise — an artefact (hash, IP, domain, file path) that indicates a system has been compromised.

content.summary: Batch analysis lets you scan an entire folder of files at once, producing a summary dashboard so you can prioritise the most suspicious files first.
content.explanation: Instead of dragging files in one at a time, batch analysis processes an entire directory — optionally including subdirectories. Each file is analysed independently and the results are collected into a single dashboard showing verdict distributions, risk-score histograms, and the top findings across all files. This workflow is essential during incident response when you receive a disk image or quarantine folder with dozens or hundreds of suspicious files. Batch analysis does not change how each individual file is examined; it simply automates the repetitive open-analyse-close cycle and adds cross-file aggregation. The dashboard highlights outliers — files with unusually high risk scores, rare packer signatures, or unique IOC patterns — so you can focus your deeper analysis where it matters most.
content.what_to_look_for: Start with the batch dashboard. Sort by risk score (descending) to see the highest-risk files first. Look for clusters of files with similar verdicts — this can indicate a campaign or a common dropper.
content.real_world_example: During the SolarWinds incident, responders received hundreds of DLLs from compromised build servers. Batch static analysis let them quickly separate the backdoored SUNBURST DLL (high entropy, unusual imports) from thousands of legitimate DLLs in the same directory.
content.next_action: Open a folder containing multiple executables using File → Analyse Directory. Review the dashboard summary before diving into any single file.

glossary.definition: Scanning multiple files in a single operation and aggregating the results.
A summary showing how many files fell into each risk category (Clean, Low, Medium, High, Critical).
Quickly assessing a large set of files to decide which ones deserve deeper investigation.

content.summary: The verdict distribution chart shows how many files in a batch fall into each risk category, giving you an instant overview of the threat landscape in that folder.
content.explanation: After batch analysis completes, the dashboard shows a distribution of verdicts: how many files scored Clean (0-9), Low (10-29), Medium (30-59), High (60-79), and Critical (80-100). A healthy software distribution should be heavily weighted toward Clean and Low. A folder where most files score Medium or above warrants urgent attention. The distribution also helps you spot anomalies: if 99 files are Clean and 1 is Critical, that outlier is your priority. Conversely, if every file scores Medium, it may indicate a systematic false positive (for example, a custom packer used by the legitimate developer) rather than a real threat. Always compare the distribution against what you expect from the source of the files.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the shape of the distribution. A normal software folder is skewed left (mostly low scores). A malware dump is skewed right. A bimodal distribution (some low, some high) may indicate a mix of legitimate tools and malicious payloads.
content.real_world_example: A SOC team analysed a USB drive confiscated during an investigation. The verdict distribution showed 47 Clean files and 3 Critical files. The 3 Critical files turned out to be a dropper, a keylogger, and a data exfiltration tool — a complete attack toolkit hidden among legitimate documents.
content.next_action: Look at the verdict distribution chart after a batch scan. Identify any files in the High or Critical categories and open them individually for deeper analysis.

glossary.definition: The risk classification assigned to a file based on its risk score (Clean, Low, Medium, High, Critical).
A file whose risk score is significantly different from the majority — often the most interesting find in a batch.
A distribution with two peaks, suggesting two distinct populations of files in the batch.

content.summary: When batch analysis flags multiple files, you need a systematic approach to triage them efficiently without missing critical threats or wasting time on false positives.
content.explanation: Multi-file triage follows the 'highest risk first' principle, but with important nuances. Step 1: Sort by risk score descending and examine Critical files first — these may need immediate containment. Step 2: Look for related files. Do multiple files share the same packer? The same C2 domain in strings? The same unusual import combination? Related files suggest a campaign rather than isolated incidents. Step 3: Check for known-good files. Hash every file and check against your allowlist and threat intelligence. Known-good files can be excluded immediately. Step 4: Group unknowns by behaviour pattern (e.g., all files with process injection APIs, all files with network indicators). Analyse one representative from each group deeply. Step 5: Document findings per-file and create an overall assessment noting any relationships between files.
content.what_to_look_for: After sorting by risk score, look for patterns: shared import combinations, shared strings (domains, IPs, registry keys), shared packer signatures, or shared section names. These relationships are more valuable than individual file scores.
content.real_world_example: During a phishing campaign investigation, batch analysis of 23 email attachments revealed 5 distinct malware families. Grouping by shared C2 domains (visible in strings) reduced the unique threats to analyse from 23 to 5, saving hours of analyst time.
content.next_action: After a batch scan with multiple flagged files, sort by score, then look at the Strings tab of the top 3 files. Note any shared domains, IPs, or file paths — these indicate files are part of the same campaign.

glossary.definition: A coordinated set of attacks using related malware, infrastructure, and techniques.
A list of known-good file hashes that can be excluded from analysis.
Finding the same IOC (hash, IP, domain) across multiple files, linking them to a common threat.

content.summary: Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) are forensic artefacts — URLs, IP addresses, file hashes, registry keys — that indicate a system may have been compromised.
content.explanation: IOCs are the breadcrumbs that malware leaves behind. They come in several types: Network indicators include URLs, domain names, and IP addresses that malware contacts for command-and-control (C2) communication or payload downloads. Host indicators include file paths where malware installs itself, registry keys it creates for persistence, mutex names it uses to prevent re-infection, and scheduled tasks it creates. Cryptographic indicators are file hashes (MD5, SHA256) that uniquely identify a specific malware sample. In static analysis, IOCs are extracted from the file's embedded strings — the readable text sequences found in the binary. Not every string is an IOC; many are legitimate library strings, error messages, or compiler artefacts. The challenge is separating the signal from the noise.
content.what_to_look_for: Open the Strings tab and look for entries tagged as IOCs. URLs and IP addresses are the most immediately actionable — you can check them against threat intelligence databases. Registry paths suggest persistence mechanisms. Base64-encoded strings may contain hidden commands or payloads.
content.real_world_example: When Emotet samples were analysed, strings extraction revealed hardcoded C2 IP addresses that rotated weekly. Sharing these IOCs through MISP (Malware Information Sharing Platform) allowed organisations worldwide to block the C2 infrastructure proactively.
content.next_action: Check the Strings tab for any entries highlighted as IOCs. Copy any URLs or IP addresses and look them up on VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, or your threat intelligence platform.

glossary.definition: Indicator of Compromise — an observable artefact that suggests a system has been breached.
Command and Control — the infrastructure attackers use to communicate with and control compromised systems.
Malware Information Sharing Platform — an open-source platform for sharing threat intelligence and IOCs.

content.summary: URLs, domains, and IP addresses found in a binary's strings are strong indicators of network communication capability — and potential C2 infrastructure.
content.explanation: When a binary contains hardcoded URLs or IP addresses, it almost certainly communicates over the network. The key question is whether that communication is legitimate (software updates, license checks, telemetry) or malicious (C2 beaconing, data exfiltration, payload downloads). Suspicious network indicators include: IP addresses in non-routable or unusual ranges, domains with high entropy names (random-looking strings like 'xk4m2n.xyz'), URLs with paths containing '/gate/', '/panel/', '/upload/', or '/cmd/' (common C2 panel patterns), and connections to Tor hidden services (.onion). Less suspicious but still noteworthy: connections to well-known cloud providers (may be legitimate or may abuse cloud infrastructure), HTTPS URLs (encrypted C2 is harder to inspect), and URLs with base64-encoded parameters (may contain encoded commands).
content.what_to_look_for: In the Strings tab, filter by IOC type 'url' or 'ip'. For each network indicator, ask: Is the domain registered recently (check WHOIS)? Does VirusTotal flag it? Is the IP in a hosting range known for bulletproof hosting? Are there multiple domains (suggesting a domain generation algorithm)?
content.real_world_example: TrickBot samples consistently contained a list of hardcoded C2 IP addresses in their strings, typically 10-20 IPs in a configuration block. Extracting these IPs from new samples and sharing them as IOCs was one of the primary ways defenders tracked and disrupted TrickBot infrastructure.
content.next_action: Copy each URL or IP address found in strings to VirusTotal (virustotal.com). Check the 'Relations' tab to see if it connects to known malware campaigns.

glossary.definition: Hosting providers that ignore abuse complaints, commonly used by cybercriminals for C2 servers.
Domain Generation Algorithm — a technique that generates new domain names algorithmically, making C2 infrastructure harder to block.
Regular, periodic network connections from malware to its C2 server, checking for commands.

content.summary: Registry paths in a binary's strings often reveal persistence mechanisms — how malware ensures it runs again after a reboot.
content.explanation: The Windows Registry is the primary persistence mechanism for most malware. Common persistence locations include: Run keys (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run) which execute a program at every login, RunOnce keys (same path but \RunOnce) which execute once then delete themselves, Services (HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services) which run as Windows services with SYSTEM privileges, Scheduled Tasks (stored in the registry under TaskCache), and COM Object hijacking (HKLM\Software\Classes\CLSID). When you see these registry paths in a binary's strings, the malware is almost certainly writing to those keys to survive reboots. The specific key reveals the persistence technique: Run keys are simple but detectable, service creation is more privileged, and COM hijacking is stealthier. Some malware uses multiple persistence mechanisms as fallbacks.
content.what_to_look_for: In the Strings tab, look for paths containing 'CurrentVersion\Run', 'CurrentControlSet\Services', 'TaskCache', or 'CLSID'. Also look for 'reg add' or 'reg.exe' strings that indicate direct registry manipulation via command line.
content.real_world_example: Agent Tesla, a commodity info-stealer, reliably writes itself to HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run with a random name. This string is always visible in static analysis and is one of the fastest ways to classify a sample as Agent Tesla.
content.next_action: Search the Strings tab for 'CurrentVersion' or 'HKLM' or 'HKCU'. Any registry paths found are likely persistence mechanisms — note them for your incident response team to check on affected systems.

glossary.definition: Techniques that allow malware to survive system reboots and maintain access to a compromised system.
A Windows Registry key that causes a program to execute automatically at user login.
Replacing a legitimate COM object registration with a malicious DLL, causing it to load when applications use that COM class.

content.summary: Each finding in Anya has a confidence level (Low, Medium, High, Critical) indicating how strongly the evidence supports a malicious interpretation.
content.explanation: Confidence levels reflect the strength of the evidence, not the severity of the threat. A Low confidence finding means the indicator has many legitimate uses (e.g., CreateFile is used by virtually all software). A Medium confidence finding has some legitimate uses but is more commonly associated with malware (e.g., SetWindowsHookEx for keylogging). A High confidence finding is rarely seen in legitimate software (e.g., NtUnmapViewOfSection for process hollowing). A Critical confidence finding is an API combination or pattern that has almost no legitimate use case (e.g., the full process injection trio of VirtualAllocEx + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread). Important: confidence is about the indicator, not the file. A file with a single Critical finding is not necessarily more dangerous than one with ten Medium findings — but the Critical finding is more reliably indicative of malicious intent.
content.what_to_look_for: In the Imports tab, note the confidence badge on each suspicious API. Focus your attention on High and Critical findings first. For Medium findings, check whether the API has a plausible legitimate use in the context of this file's apparent purpose.
content.real_world_example: A developer's build tool was flagged with Medium confidence for importing VirtualAlloc and WriteProcessMemory — used legitimately for JIT compilation. However, it also had a Critical confidence finding for CreateRemoteThread, which had no legitimate explanation. The Critical finding was the true indicator; the Medium findings were false positives in context.
content.next_action: Review the confidence levels in the Imports tab. For any High or Critical findings, read the associated MITRE technique description to understand exactly what capability the finding implies.

glossary.definition: A rating (Low, Medium, High, Critical) of how strongly an indicator suggests malicious behaviour.
A legitimate file or behaviour incorrectly flagged as suspicious.
A technique where malware creates a suspended legitimate process, replaces its code, then resumes it.

content.summary: A file with many Medium-confidence findings can be just as suspicious as one with a single High-confidence finding — the combination of capabilities matters.
content.explanation: Individual Medium-confidence APIs often have legitimate uses: VirtualAlloc (memory management), CreateToolhelp32Snapshot (diagnostic tools), InternetOpen (update checkers). But when a single file imports all three, the picture changes. VirtualAlloc + WriteProcessMemory suggests in-memory payload manipulation. Add CreateToolhelp32Snapshot and it looks like process enumeration before injection. Add InternetOpen and the file can download additional payloads. Each API alone is Medium; together they describe a complete attack chain. Anya's risk score reflects this accumulation — each Medium finding adds 8 points, so five Medium findings produce a score of 40, which is solidly in the Medium risk range. This additive approach captures the intuition that breadth of suspicious capability is itself suspicious, even when no single indicator is definitive.
content.what_to_look_for: When the risk score is elevated but no single finding is High or Critical, look at the full list of Medium findings. Can you construct a narrative where these capabilities work together? If the APIs describe a coherent attack workflow (enumerate → allocate → write → execute → communicate), treat the combination as High confidence.
content.real_world_example: A fileless malware loader was missed by signature-based AV because no single API import was Critical. However, it imported 7 Medium-confidence APIs that together described: process enumeration, memory allocation with execute permissions, shellcode writing, thread creation, and HTTP download. The accumulated risk score of 56 correctly flagged it for analyst review.
content.next_action: List all Medium-confidence findings from the Imports tab. Try to construct an attack narrative from them. If they form a coherent chain, mentally upgrade the overall assessment to High.

glossary.definition: A sequence of techniques used together to achieve a malicious objective.
Malware that operates entirely in memory without writing traditional files to disk.
A risk scoring method where each finding adds points, and the total reflects the cumulative suspiciousness.

content.summary: Analysis thresholds control when Anya flags something as suspicious. Adjusting them lets you reduce noise or increase sensitivity depending on your environment.
content.explanation: Anya uses configurable thresholds to decide when an indicator is worth flagging. The main thresholds include: Entropy threshold — the Shannon entropy value above which a section is flagged as potentially packed or encrypted (default: 7.0). Import suspicion level — the minimum confidence level to display in the Imports tab (default: Medium, meaning Low-confidence findings are hidden). Risk score alert level — the score above which a file is highlighted as needing attention (default: 40). String minimum length — the minimum character count for extracted strings (default: 6). These defaults work well for general-purpose malware analysis, but they may need adjustment. If you analyse a lot of packed commercial software (game protectors, DRM), you might raise the entropy threshold to reduce false positives. If you are performing deep forensic analysis, you might lower thresholds to catch subtle indicators.
content.what_to_look_for: Open Settings → Thresholds. Note the current values. If you are seeing too many false positives, consider raising the entropy or risk score thresholds. If you are missing findings, lower them.
content.real_world_example: A game security company raised their entropy threshold to 7.8 because all their software used Themida packing (entropy ~7.5). This eliminated hundreds of false positives per day while still catching truly anomalous samples with entropy above 7.8.
content.next_action: Open Settings → Thresholds and review the current values. Make a note of the defaults before changing anything so you can revert if needed.

glossary.definition: A configurable boundary value that determines when an indicator is flagged as suspicious.
How readily the analyser flags indicators — higher sensitivity catches more but produces more false positives.
False positive findings that clutter the results and distract from genuine threats.

content.summary: Different environments need different thresholds. A SOC analysing enterprise software has different needs than a malware research lab.
content.explanation: Threshold tuning is an iterative process. Start with defaults and adjust based on your false positive and false negative rates. For enterprise SOC environments: raise the risk score alert threshold to 50-60 if you receive many legitimate but flagged admin tools (PsExec, WinSCP, PowerShell scripts). Keep entropy threshold at default (7.0) unless you have specific packer-heavy software. For malware research labs: lower the string minimum length to 4 to catch short but meaningful strings like 'cmd' or 'reg'. Lower the risk score alert to 20 to catch subtle indicators. Keep entropy threshold at default or lower to 6.5 for detecting partial encryption. For incident response: use aggressive (low) thresholds — you want maximum sensitivity because you already know something is wrong. False positives are acceptable when you are hunting for IOCs across a compromised environment. Always document your threshold changes and the reasoning behind them. Review thresholds quarterly as your environment and threat landscape change.
content.what_to_look_for: Track your false positive rate over a week with the current thresholds. If more than 30% of flagged files turn out to be legitimate, your thresholds are too sensitive. If you miss known-bad files, they are not sensitive enough.
content.real_world_example: An MSSP (Managed Security Service Provider) maintained three threshold profiles: 'Enterprise' (high thresholds, low noise) for routine monitoring, 'Hunting' (medium thresholds) for proactive threat hunting, and 'IR' (lowest thresholds, maximum sensitivity) activated during active incident response engagements.
content.next_action: Create a baseline: analyse 10 known-good files and 5 known-bad files with your current thresholds. Note any false positives or false negatives, then adjust accordingly.

glossary.definition: The percentage of legitimate files incorrectly flagged as suspicious.
The percentage of malicious files that were not flagged as suspicious.
Managed Security Service Provider — a company that provides outsourced security monitoring and management.

content.summary: Anya categorises extracted strings by IOC type — URLs, IPs, registry paths, base64, file paths — so you can quickly find the most relevant indicators.
content.explanation: A typical binary contains hundreds or thousands of strings, most of which are uninteresting (compiler-generated strings, library names, format strings). Anya automatically classifies strings into IOC categories to help you focus on what matters. URL strings may reveal C2 servers, download locations, or exfiltration endpoints. IP addresses can identify hardcoded C2 infrastructure. Registry paths suggest persistence or configuration storage mechanisms. Base64-encoded strings often contain hidden commands, encoded payloads, or obfuscated configuration data — decoding them can reveal the malware's true intent. File paths indicate where the malware reads from or writes to on disk. By filtering to a specific IOC type, you can quickly scan just the network indicators, just the persistence indicators, or just the encoded content, making triage much faster than reading every string sequentially.
content.what_to_look_for: In the Strings tab, use the IOC type filter to view one category at a time. Start with URLs and IPs (most immediately actionable), then check registry paths (persistence), then base64 (hidden content). Decode any base64 strings using CyberChef or a command-line tool.
content.real_world_example: A QakBot sample contained 2,300 extracted strings. Filtering by IOC type 'url' reduced this to 4 strings — all C2 panel URLs. Filtering by 'base64' revealed an encoded PowerShell command that downloaded the second-stage payload. Without filtering, these critical strings were buried in noise.
content.next_action: Open the Strings tab and use the IOC type filter dropdown. Select 'url' first and review any findings. Then try 'base64' — if you find encoded strings, decode them with CyberChef (gchq.github.io/CyberChef).

glossary.definition: An encoding scheme that represents binary data as ASCII text — commonly used to obfuscate malicious commands in strings.
A free web-based tool by GCHQ for encoding, decoding, and transforming data in many formats.
The process of finding readable text sequences in a binary file, typically sequences of 4+ printable ASCII characters.

content.summary: Not every flagged file is malicious. Learning to recognise common false positive patterns prevents wasted time and alert fatigue.
content.explanation: False positives are legitimate files that trigger suspicious indicators. The most common patterns include: System administration tools (PsExec, WinSCP, PuTTY) that import network and process manipulation APIs for legitimate purposes. Development tools and debuggers that use the same anti-debug and memory manipulation APIs as malware. Packed commercial software (games with anti-cheat, DRM-protected applications) that triggers high-entropy and packer-detection alerts. Installers and self-extracting archives that contain overlay data and import file-writing APIs. Go and Rust binaries that statically link many libraries, inflating the import count with functions they never actually use maliciously. Security software itself (AV engines, EDR agents) that imports the same hooking and injection APIs as malware because they use similar techniques for detection. The key to identifying false positives is context: does the file's apparent purpose explain the suspicious indicators?
content.what_to_look_for: Before classifying a file as malicious, check: Is it digitally signed by a reputable publisher? Is the file from a known software package? Do the suspicious APIs make sense for the file's stated purpose? Is the file a known tool (check the hash on VirusTotal — a 0/70 detection ratio strongly suggests legitimate software).
content.real_world_example: A SOC repeatedly escalated SysInternals Process Explorer as malicious because it imports CreateRemoteThread, ReadProcessMemory, and NtQueryInformationProcess. After adding its hash to the allowlist, they eliminated approximately 15 false positive tickets per week.
content.next_action: If a file scores Medium (30-59) but looks like it could be legitimate, check its hash on VirusTotal. If it has 0 detections and is signed, it is very likely a false positive. Add it to your notes for allowlisting.

glossary.definition: The phenomenon where analysts become desensitised to alerts due to a high volume of false positives.
A list of known-good file hashes or publishers that are excluded from suspicious flagging.
A cryptographic signature proving a file was published by a specific entity and has not been tampered with.

content.summary: A valid digital signature does not guarantee a file is safe. Stolen certificates, supply chain compromises, and dual-use tools all carry valid signatures while posing real threats.
content.explanation: Code signing establishes that a file was published by a specific entity and has not been modified since signing. It does NOT prove the file is benign. There are several scenarios where signed files are genuinely dangerous: Stolen code-signing certificates — attackers steal private keys from compromised developers and sign their malware. The signature is technically valid, but the key is in the wrong hands. Supply chain attacks — an attacker compromises the build pipeline of a legitimate vendor, injecting malware into an otherwise legitimate signed update (SolarWinds, CCleaner, ASUS Live Update). Dual-use tools — legitimate signed tools like PsExec, Cobalt Strike (licensed versions), and PowerShell are used by attackers for lateral movement and post-exploitation. The tool is genuinely from the stated publisher, but its presence on a system may indicate compromise. Expired or revoked certificates — a file may have been signed with a certificate that has since been revoked due to compromise, but the signature still validates if timestamp-checked before revocation.
content.what_to_look_for: If a signed file has a high risk score, check: Is the signing certificate still valid and not revoked? Is the publisher name consistent with the file's apparent purpose? Is the signing timestamp recent? Does the file hash appear on threat intelligence platforms despite being signed? A signed file with process injection APIs and no legitimate reason for them is a red flag regardless of the signature.
content.real_world_example: The SolarWinds SUNBURST backdoor was signed with SolarWinds' legitimate code-signing certificate. The signature was valid, the publisher was trusted, and the file was distributed through the official update channel. Only detailed static and behavioural analysis revealed the embedded backdoor, which would have been missed by any tool that trusted signatures alone.
content.next_action: For signed files with elevated risk scores: verify the certificate validity (check for revocation), cross-reference the hash on VirusTotal, and examine the suspicious APIs to determine if they are consistent with the software's stated purpose.

glossary.definition: Cryptographically signing a binary to prove its publisher identity and integrity.
Compromising a trusted vendor's build or distribution pipeline to inject malware into legitimate software updates.
The process of invalidating a code-signing certificate, typically after a compromise is discovered.

content.summary: A file-type mismatch means the file's actual content does not match its extension — a classic social engineering technique to trick users into running executables.
content.explanation: Every file format has 'magic bytes' — the first few bytes that identify what the file actually is. A PE executable starts with 'MZ' (4D 5A), a PDF starts with '%PDF', a ZIP starts with 'PK' (50 4B). When the magic bytes say 'this is a PE executable' but the file extension says '.pdf' or '.docx', someone has deliberately renamed the file to disguise its true nature. This is one of the oldest and most common social engineering techniques: an attacker sends 'invoice.pdf.exe' in an email. Windows hides known extensions by default, so the user sees 'invoice.pdf' and double-clicks it, executing the malware. More sophisticated variants use Right-to-Left Override (RLO) Unicode characters to visually reverse part of the filename, making 'report_fdp.exe' appear as 'report_exe.pdf' in the file manager. Anya compares the detected file format against the original extension and flags any mismatch.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the Overview tab for a file-type mismatch warning. Check the detected format (PE, ELF, ZIP, etc.) against the original filename extension. Any mismatch is suspicious, but executable-masquerading-as-document is a strong indicator of social engineering delivery.
content.real_world_example: During a targeted attack on a law firm, attackers sent emails with attachments named 'settlement_agreement.pdf.exe'. Windows' default 'hide known extensions' setting made these appear as PDF files. Anya's file-type mismatch detection immediately flagged the PE magic bytes inside a supposed PDF.
content.next_action: If a file-type mismatch is detected, treat the file with high suspicion regardless of its risk score. Report the original filename and true file type in your triage notes.

glossary.definition: The first few bytes of a file that identify its true format, regardless of the file extension.
Right-to-Left Override — a Unicode control character that reverses text direction, abused to disguise file extensions.
Manipulating people into performing actions or divulging information, often by disguising malicious files as legitimate documents.

content.summary: An attack chain is a sequence of techniques that together achieve a complex objective. Recognising the chain reveals the attacker's full intent, not just individual capabilities.
content.explanation: Malware rarely uses a single technique in isolation. Real attacks follow chains: Delivery → Execution → Persistence → Privilege Escalation → Discovery → Lateral Movement → Collection → Exfiltration. In static analysis, you can often reconstruct parts of this chain from the import table and strings. A process injection chain might look like: CreateToolhelp32Snapshot (enumerate processes to find a target) → OpenProcess (get a handle to the target) → VirtualAllocEx (allocate memory in the target) → WriteProcessMemory (write shellcode) → CreateRemoteThread (execute the shellcode). A persistence chain might be: GetModuleFileName (find own path) → CopyFile (copy self to a persistent location) → RegSetValueEx (create a Run key). A data exfiltration chain: FindFirstFile/FindNextFile (enumerate files) → CreateFile/ReadFile (read target files) → InternetOpen/HttpSendRequest (upload to C2). The power of chain recognition is that it reveals intent: individual APIs are ambiguous, but the chain tells you exactly what the malware is trying to do.
content.what_to_look_for: In the Imports tab, look for APIs that form logical sequences. Group them mentally: Which APIs are for process manipulation? Which are for file operations? Which are for networking? Which are for registry access? Each group represents one step in the attack chain.
content.real_world_example: APT28 (Fancy Bear) X-Agent implant showed a clear three-stage attack chain in static analysis: Stage 1 — process injection (VirtualAllocEx chain), Stage 2 — credential harvesting (LsaEnumerateLogonSessions chain), Stage 3 — exfiltration (InternetOpen chain). Mapping these chains to MITRE ATT&CK produced a complete attack narrative that matched known APT28 TTPs.
content.next_action: List all suspicious APIs from the Imports tab. Group them by function (process, memory, network, registry, file). For each group, identify the likely purpose. Then connect the groups into a narrative: what is this malware trying to do, step by step?

glossary.definition: A sequence of techniques used together to achieve a complex malicious objective.
A military-derived model (Lockheed Martin) describing the stages of a cyberattack from reconnaissance to action on objectives.
Moving from one compromised system to another within a network to expand access.
